text: Single-recision robotic colon cancer surgery using the X-eye robotic system. Maximum utilization of endo-wrist function. This is a forty-one-year-old female with initial presentation of blood issue. Digital rectal estimation showed tinged blood at the fingertip. Colonoscopy revealed an ulcerative mass, twenty centimeters from the anal verge. Biosphere report showed adenocarcinoma with elevated level of CA up to four hundred and eighty-eight. Under the diagnosis, colon cancer with preoperative staging of T-three and two M-X. Single incision robotic surgery plasma was arranged for this patient.
Initial colonoscopy showed an ulcerative mass with easy touch bleeding at the sycamore column. Optimal CT showed an outmass lesion at the sigmoid column indicated by red arrow.

The surgical team sets up for single incision robotic surgery plus one, placing the main growth port at the umbilicus and inserting three 8mm robotic trocars. Bipolar forceps are used for arm one, a robotic camera for arm two, and polarized forceps for arm three. A conventional laparoscopic trocar is placed at the right lower quadrant, with a forced robotic trocar inserted inside it, demonstrating the trocar-in-trocar technique. The robotic machine is positioned on the left side of the patient, utilizing the latest version of the X-eye system.

The surgeon carefully dissects the soft tissue from the inter-aortic triangle to preserve the hypogastric nerve. Dissection continues along the ventral side of the abdominal aorta, with gentle traction around the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). The hypogastric nerve is preserved at the inter-aortic triangle and near the IMA root. The IMA is dissected and transected 1-2 centimeters away from the root, with soft tissues separated and pushed down. A hemoclip is applied for double clipping on the proximal side, and a 10mm endoclip is used on the distal side of the IMA.

The surgeon uses an energy device to transect the vessel, followed by dissection along the areolar tissue between the sigmoid mesocolon and the gel autophagia. The medial approach is employed, providing ample working space with minimal handling of the sigmoid colon. The dissection reveals the ureter, dumbbells, and long dorsal vessels, continuing at the transition from the mesosigmoid to the mesorectum to ensure safe margins for transection. Dissection proceeds along the left pelvic side, preserving the hypogastric nerve at the sacral promontory level, which lies one to two centimeters medial to the ureter.

The robotic excise system is used to clear soft tissue surrounding the sigmoid colon. The surgeon prepares the GIA stapler for transection, ensuring no surrounding structures are trapped. The proximal and distal ends of the sigmoid colon stump are checked for integrity. The final specimen reveals a 5 cm ulcerating mass with adequate proximal and distal margins, confirming a T3 N2 M0 staging.
